topic: goAsync extends the window slightly, without becoming a Service
question: Q4 and Q7 established that onReceive should be brief and delegate real work elsewhere. Where does `goAsync()` fit into that relationship?
choices: goAsync() turns the receiver into a full Service with an unlimited execution window

goAsync() is a middle-ground tool — it grants a modest, still-limited amount of extra time beyond onReceive's normal brief window, for small amounts of additional work that don't justify starting a whole separate Service, while still requiring an explicit finish() to tell the OS the receiver is truly done
goAsync() has no real effect and exists purely as documentation
goAsync() permanently registers this receiver so it never needs to be redeclared

explanation.summary: This refines Q4 and Q7's 'onReceive must be brief, delegate real work to a Service' guidance with a specific middle-ground tool: goAsync() extends the window somewhat, for cases where the work is slightly more than instantaneous but still doesn't warrant the overhead of an entire Service.
lineByLine: `goAsync()` — signals to the OS 'I need a bit more time than a normal onReceive gets, but I'm not becoming a long-running Service.'
The extra time granted is still modest and bounded — it does NOT turn this receiver into something with a Service's open-ended lifetime (Chapter 8).
`pendingResult.finish()` — must be called explicitly once the extra work is done, telling the OS this receiver's job really is complete now.
explanation.tip: Think of the options as three tiers: normal onReceive (near-instant), goAsync() (a little more time, still bounded), and starting a Service (Chapter 8, for genuinely long-running work). Choosing the right tier is a relationship between how much time the reaction actually needs and how much overhead is appropriate to take on.

topic: ordered broadcasts — sequential, not simultaneous fan-out
snippet:
Q1 described broadcasts as many independent receivers all
reacting to the same event, with no particular relationship
between them.

An ORDERED broadcast is different: multiple receivers are
given a priority order, called ONE AT A TIME in sequence;
any one of them can choose to abort the broadcast,
preventing lower-priority receivers from ever being called
at all.
question: How does an ordered broadcast's relationship between its receivers differ from the simultaneous, independent relationship described in Q1?
choices: There's no real difference — all broadcasts, ordered or not, behave identically

Unlike Q1's simultaneous, mutually-independent receivers, an ordered broadcast's receivers form a sequential chain based on priority, where an earlier receiver can deliberately stop the broadcast from ever reaching later, lower-priority receivers — introducing a dependency between receivers that a normal broadcast doesn't have
Ordered broadcasts can only ever have exactly one receiver
Aborting an ordered broadcast also cancels every previously-called receiver's already-completed work

explanation.summary: This nuances Q1's foundational 'one-to-many, independent' model: ordered broadcasts introduce genuine dependency and sequencing between receivers, which is structurally different from the simultaneous fan-out relationship described earlier in the chapter.
lineByLine: Q1's normal broadcast: every matching receiver reacts independently, with no receiver aware of or able to affect any other.
Ordered broadcast: receivers are called ONE AT A TIME, highest priority first.

Any receiver in that chain can call `abortBroadcast()`, and lower-priority receivers further down the chain will never be invoked at all for this particular broadcast — a real dependency that a normal broadcast's receivers simply don't have on each other.
explanation.tip: This distinction matters when reading real code: seeing `android:priority` on an intent-filter, or `abortBroadcast()` inside onReceive, signals you're looking at this more structured, sequential relationship — not the simpler, fully-independent fan-out that's the default assumption throughout the rest of this chapter.
